content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Early selection at 10–12 into 6/8-grade gimnaziums is a documented, non-trivial contributor to Hungary's exceptionally high SES/ethnic school segregation, but its pedagogical value-added is heterogeneous by track (stronger for 8-grade than 6-grade), so the equity case against early selection is stronger than the achievement case for it.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: Hungary's SES-achievement gradient is among the largest in the OECD, and between-school variance in math performance is high, with schools selecting students based on records.
Hungary has the highest socioeconomic school segregation in the EU, and the parent-education achievement gap (53 PISA points) is far above the EU average (33 points).
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: We do not have recent (2020s) Hungary-specific causal estimates isolating the pure selection effect of the 6/8-grade gate from confounding factors (family investment, self-selection, prior ability) using post-2020 data.

It is unclear how much of the observed peer-effect/compositional damage to non-selected pupils is caused specifically by the 6/8-grade gate versus by free primary-school choice and church/private school growth operating independently of age-10/12 tracking.
